advaita.rendering: Among the Vṛṣṇis I am Vāsudeva — your very companion here — and among the Pāṇḍavas you yourself, Dhanañjaya, are my vibhūti (divine manifestation). Among the munis (sages who reflect on all knowable objects) I am Vyāsa, and among the kavis (far-seeing seers) I am the poet Uśanas.
advaita.doctrinal_note: Śaṅkara reads this list flatly as instances of the highest within each class — the examples do not privilege personal devotion but illustrate that the one Brahman shines most luminously wherever reflective intelligence (manana) and penetrating vision (krāntadarśana) are most concentrated. The self-identification 'your companion' (tvatsakhā) signals that the teacher of jñāna stands here as an equal, not a lord over a servant.
advaita.key_divergence: No bhakti-rasa is drawn from this verse; the vibhūtis are pedagogical pointers toward the universal Ātman, not occasions for surrender.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: The fact that Bhagavān took birth as Vāsudeva, son of Vasudeva, is itself a vibhūti (auspicious divine attribute) — there is no other purpose for it. Among the Pāṇḍavas I am Arjuna; among munis (those who perceive the true nature of objects through manana) I am Vyāsa; and among the kavis (the discerning wise) I am present as the supreme.
viśiṣṭādvaita.doctrinal_note: Rāmānuja inserts vasudeva-sūnutvam (sonship of Vasudeva) as a real vibhūti, not a mere symbol — the divine descent itself demonstrates Bhagavān's perfect auspicious qualities (kalyāṇaguṇas). The mention of Arjuna as vibhūti reinforces that a bhakta who has offered himself completely becomes a locus of the Lord's own radiance.
viśiṣṭādvaita.key_divergence: Where Advaita reads 'companion' as equality, Viśiṣṭādvaita reads it as the Lord lovingly permeating and sustaining the devotee from within — antarātman (inner controller), not identity.

dvaita.rendering: Vāsudeva means the one who covers all, inhabits all, and in whom all reside — this name encodes that Hari alone is the independent, all-pervading reality. Vyāsa means the one who is distinct from (viśiṣṭa) everything, present everywhere simultaneously, as confirmed in the Mokṣadharma and the Āgniveśya-śākhā: 'Nārāyaṇa, pervading within and without, fills everything that is seen or heard in this entire world.'
dvaita.doctrinal_note: Madhva anchors each name etymologically in Hari's absolute independence (svatantratā) and all-pervasiveness. The cross-references to Mokṣadharma and Āgniveśya-śākhā are characteristic Dvaita polemic: scripture itself proves that Vāsudeva and Vyāsa are not titles of elevated jīvas but names of the one eternally distinct Supreme who is never identical with any finite self.
dvaita.key_divergence: Madhva explicitly uses this verse to block the Advaita reading — the vibhūtis prove Hari's ubiquitous dominance over eternally subordinate jīvas, not a non-dual identity.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Even among the Yādavas, the Lord declares his vibhūti-pervasion: 'I am the one characterized by the dharma of Vāsudeva-hood — born into that form, I am the one to be meditated upon.' For the Pāṇḍavas, Dhanañjaya is I myself, since Nara (Arjuna) is famously the aṃśa (portion) of Nārāyaṇa. Among the munis who contemplate the meaning of the Veda I am Kṛṣṇadvaipāyana; Uśanas is Śukra.
śuddhādvaita.doctrinal_note: Vallabha reads the Vāsudeva vibhūti as Bhagavān's own līlā (playful self-manifestation as Puruṣottama) descending into the Yadu clan — the birth itself is prasāda (grace), not an ordinary event. The Nara-Nārāyaṇa identification of Arjuna elevates the devotee into the Lord's own playfield: the entire Kurukṣetra scene is Kṛṣṇa's drama, with his own aṃśa playing opposite him.
śuddhādvaita.key_divergence: The verse is primarily about ānanda-filled self-revelation in the context of divine sport, not a philosophical taxonomy of excellence. Every name vibrates with the joy of Kṛṣṇa recognizing his own play.

bhakti.rendering: I am the Vāsudeva who is here teaching you; you, Dhanañjaya, are my very vibhūti among the Pāṇḍavas. Among the munis, those devoted to reflecting (manana) on the meaning of the Veda, I am Veda-Vyāsa. Among the kavis, the seers of poetic vision, I am the poet named Uśanas — that is, Śukra.
bhakti.doctrinal_note: Śrīdhara Svāmī's commentary is concise and anchored in the text's immediate meaning: the Lord points to himself as teacher (tvām upadiśāmi — 'I who teach you'), grounding the vibhūti-list in the devotional relationship already established. Vyāsa-ship is glossed specifically as veda-artha-manana (reflection on Vedic meaning), not generic sage-wisdom.
bhakti.key_divergence: No elaborate doctrinal apparatus is invoked; the bhakti reading holds that recognizing these vibhūtis as the Lord's own forms naturally awakens love (bhāva) and deepens the student's receptivity to the teaching being transmitted right now.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: It was stated earlier that even the Supreme Lord's own vibhūtis are included in this list so that He may be contemplated in those forms. Among the Vṛṣṇis, I am Vāsudeva — famous as son of Vasudeva — the very one who teaches you. Among the Pāṇḍavas, Dhanañjaya is I. Among the munis, even those of profound reflection (manana), I am Veda-Vyāsa. Among the kavis, the far-sighted discerners of subtle meaning, I am the one known as the poet Uśanas, that is Śukra.
advaita-bhakti.doctrinal_note: Madhusūdana holds both registers simultaneously: the gloss 'sūkṣmārtha-vivekī' (discerner of subtle meaning) for kavi points toward the jñānin who pierces māyā, while 'tvad-upadeṣṭā ayam aham' (this one who teaches you is I) retains the intense personal bhakti-relation. The list of vibhūtis is thus a meditation-ladder: one begins with love for the named form and ascends to non-dual recognition.
advaita-bhakti.key_divergence: Madhusūdana's synthesis is intentional — where pure Advaita discards rasa once jñāna dawns, he insists that the rasa of Kṛṣṇa-bhakti itself becomes the vehicle of Advaita-realization, not its obstacle.

so_what_questions: Why does Kṛṣṇa list himself alongside a human student (Arjuna) as a vibhūti — does this collapse the teacher-student distinction or deepen it?
Vyāsa compiled the Mahābhārata containing this very verse: what is the significance of Kṛṣṇa claiming to be the very author of the text in which this claim appears?
All four names (Vāsudeva, Dhanañjaya, Vyāsa, Uśanas) carry distinct etymological weights — is the verse also a lesson in how names encode the divine?
Uśanas/Śukra is the guru of the asuras (demons): what does it mean for the Lord to identify with the supreme intelligence even on the 'opposing' side?
If excellence in any domain is a vibhūti of the Lord, does striving for mastery become a form of worship — or does that logic risk spiritual inflation?
Arjuna is named here as Dhanañjaya ('conqueror of wealth'): given that this conversation is about to send him into battle, what burden does being a divine vibhūti place on a human being?

All six schools agree the verse points toward the highest in each category — but they disagree on whether 'highest' means identity with Brahman, transparent servanthood, or something else. What is at stake in that disagreement for how one lives?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter a person of exceptional clarity or penetrating understanding, instead of projecting envy or admiration onto them as a separate self, practice recognizing that brilliance as the same Ātman that is your own ground. The vibhūti-list is an exercise in seeing through surfaces to the single luminous awareness behind them.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Your unique gifts — intelligence, strength, the capacity to help others — are not self-generated; they are Bhagavān permeating you from within as your antarātman (inner controller). Dedicate each use of those gifts explicitly back to the Lord: this is the kainkarya (loving service) that Rāmānuja's school calls the highest life.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Whatever domain you excel in, remember that your excellence is on loan from Hari and returns to nothing if he withdraws his support. Cultivating genuine humility — not performance humility — is the practical corollary of Madhva's insistence on jīva-dependence (pāratantryam). Check daily: am I operating as a servant of a greater will, or as an autonomous source?
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's reading invites you to experience your own best moments — a creative flow state, a moment of athletic precision, a burst of genuine kindness — as Kṛṣṇa's own līlā happening through you. The practice is to hold that recognition lightly and gratefully in the moment itself, not retrospectively. Joy in excellence is itself prasāda (grace freely given).
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's plain reading points to a simple discipline: identify the teachers in your life who are genuinely illumining you right now, and receive their instruction with the same attentiveness Arjuna brings here. The Lord speaks through actual human guides; honoring that relationship is a primary bhakti practice.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's synthesis suggests a meditation practice: choose one domain where you sense a genuine calling (writing, music, service, scholarship), contemplate the Kṛṣṇa-vibhūti behind the greatest exemplars of that domain, then let that love and awe draw you toward your own deepest capacity. Bhakti and excellence are not competitors — devotional intensity fuels mastery.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas were present and clean; full doctrinal divergence achieved, especially on the Vāsudeva-etymology (Madhva), the Nara-Nārāyaṇa identification (Vallabha), and the jñāna-vs-bhakti graduation (Madhusūdana); Śukra-as-asura-guru flagged in so-what questions; no HTML artifacts; no English suffixes on Sanskrit roots.
